Source: [here](https://mravinderreddy.substack.com/p/the-arc-where-it-began).

The Arc Where It Began

# The Arc Where It Began

### The Indo-European homeland was not the steppe. It was the arc of Zagros, Armenia, and Anatolia, the highland rim of the northern Fertile Crescent. Call it ZAA.

[](https://substack.com/@mravinderreddy)

![Ravinder Reddy's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2Tvm!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F77da9531-28fb-41f8-b757-599e5b73f4c6_1202x1204.png)

[Ravinder Reddy](https://substack.com/@mravinderreddy)

Jun 06, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

5

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5q-D!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe586db15-55ea-46ae-bf05-e615d2d00668_2916x1608.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5q-D!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe586db15-55ea-46ae-bf05-e615d2d00668_2916x1608.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The highland arc at dawn, a Neolithic temple standing above its village in the country where farming and the gods began.

The standard story is that the Indo-European languages, the family that now includes English, Hindi, Persian, Greek, Russian, and Latin, came galloping out of the Pontic-Caspian steppe around 3,500 BCE on horseback. That story is half right, and the half it gets wrong is the most important half. The horsemen were real. The expansion was real. But they were the delivery truck, not the factory. The factory sat fifteen hundred kilometers to the south, in a belt of highland country that I will call the arc: **Zagros, Armenia, Anatolia**, the northern rim of the Fertile Crescent.

This essay makes the case for that arc from the ground up. I am going to lean hard on one rhetorical advantage along the way. The strongest single body of evidence for a southern homeland was produced by the people most committed to the steppe, the labs of David Reich, Iosif Lazaridis, and David Anthony. I do not share their conclusions. I do think their data convicts them. When a hostile witness keeps handing you receipts, you take the receipts.

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Subscribe

Start where everything starts. With farming.

## I. The first revolution happened in the arc

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Around 9,500 BCE, in the hills of the northern Fertile Crescent, people stopped chasing food and started growing it. Wheat and barley were pulled out of wild grasses on those slopes. Lentils, peas, chickpeas, flax. Goats and sheep were folded into human life within a few centuries and a few hundred kilometers of each other, all of it inside the arc. Not one discovery in one place but a slow regional convergence across the whole crescent.

Before the grain was even fully tame, the people of the arc were building on a scale no one had attempted before. At **Göbekli Tepe**, in southeastern Anatolia, between roughly 9,600 and 8,000 BCE, hunter-gatherers who had barely begun to farm quarried limestone pillars weighing up to ten tons, carved them into great T-shapes, decorated them with foxes and snakes and vultures and scorpions, and stood them in rings. Then they buried them and built more. This is the oldest monumental sacred architecture on Earth, seven thousand years older than Stonehenge, older than pottery, older than the wheel, older than writing by six millennia, and older than agriculture itself. The temple came before the farm.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hUrP!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d9d0857-b5bb-41d2-8e04-f953ddc3ff0c_1920x1275.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!hUrP!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d9d0857-b5bb-41d2-8e04-f953ddc3ff0c_1920x1275.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Göbekli Tepe, southeastern Anatolia, raised between 9,600 and 8,000 BCE. Hunter-gatherers carved and stood these ten-ton pillars before pottery, the wheel, or farming. The temple came before the farm.

A few hundred kilometers and a thousand years away, at **Çatalhöyük** on the Konya plain, several thousand people packed into a honeycomb of mud-brick houses with no streets, entering through holes in the roofs and walking home across their neighbors’ rooftops. They set the horn cores of wild bulls into their walls. They modeled plaster breasts over the skulls of vultures and the jaws of foxes. They buried their dead under the sleeping platforms, and sometimes dug the skulls back up, packed the faces with plaster, and kept them in circulation among the living. Ian Hodder’s decades at the site have documented all of it.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BHIe!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb73c83b4-08ea-48de-91fd-930e628d76ec_1666x1738.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BHIe!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb73c83b4-08ea-48de-91fd-930e628d76ec_1666x1738.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*The Seated Woman of Çatalhöyük, around 6,000 BCE. A goddess between two leopards, from a town of bull horns and plastered ancestor skulls.*

By 7,000 BCE the Anatolian farmers were a dense, surplus-producing civilization of mud-brick towns, thousands of people each, trading obsidian and weaving cloth. A growing farming population does one thing reliably. It runs out of land.

## II. The farmers moved, and we can trace the routes

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Around 6,700 BCE they began leaving, and they went two ways.

One stream took the water. Aegean farmers moved west island by island, Crete and the Cyclades and the Greek mainland, carrying wheat and goats and pottery in small boats across the short blue gaps of the Aegean. The other stream walked, pushing up out of Greece into the Balkans along the river valleys, following the Vardar and the Struma into the heart of the continent. By 6,000 BCE farming had reached the Carpathian basin on the middle Danube.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5D7A!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7920b7c6-a1ad-4975-b10f-c4ae8b73dae8_2158x1373.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5D7A!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7920b7c6-a1ad-4975-b10f-c4ae8b73dae8_2158x1373.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

How farming spread out of the arc between 9,600 and 4,000 BCE, west across the Aegean and up the Balkan rivers into Europe, and east along the Zagros. The first farmers came in person. Map: Gronenborn, Horejs, Börner and Ober 2023, CC BY 4.0.

There the wave stalled for a thousand years. North of the Danube the soil turns heavy, cold, and wet, nothing like the light dry Mediterranean hillsides the Aegean toolkit was built for. Southern farming did not work on it. So the frontier sat pinned against the edge of the Hungarian plain for ten centuries, until a culture called the Linearbandkeramik finally cracked the loess around 5,500 BCE and the frontier surged again, this time all the way to the Atlantic.

That pause matters because of who was waiting on the far side of it, and because of what ancient DNA later revealed about the meeting.

## III. The first farmers were not the people already there

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For decades, the spread of farming into Europe was an unsettled question. Did people move and carry farming with them, or did Europe’s old hunter-gatherers simply pick up the new techniques and stay put?

Ancient DNA ended the argument, and the answer was migration. When the first genomes came out of early European farmer skeletons, beginning with Haak and colleagues around 2010 and then Mathieson and colleagues with hundreds of ancient individuals in 2015, Europe’s first farmers turned out to be a distinct people whose closest match was the Neolithic population of Anatolia. They came from the arc, and they came in person.

The two populations did not even look alike. The hunter-gatherers had held Europe for fourteen thousand continuous years, and their genomes describe a face that has almost vanished today: dark skin with blue or pale eyes. The seven-thousand-year-old man from Loschbour and the pair from La Braña in Spain carried exactly that combination. The incoming farmers were close to the reverse, carrying the derived variant of the pigmentation gene **SLC24A5**, the single strongest light-skin allele in West Eurasia, at around ninety-nine percent, with a tendency toward lighter skin and darker eyes. The modern European face did not exist yet. It had to be mixed, over thousands of years, out of these two strangers meeting at the edge of the loess.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!w3UK!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9d654779-84d8-466d-9b0f-3b93e805fe3c_1920x1280.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!w3UK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9d654779-84d8-466d-9b0f-3b93e805fe3c_1920x1280.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Cheddar Man, a 10,000-year-old Mesolithic Briton, reconstructed from his genome with dark skin and pale eyes. He belongs to the same Western hunter-gatherer population as La Braña and Loschbour, the people Europe's first farmers met and replaced. Reconstruction by Kennis and Kennis, photo via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 2.0.

One detail is worth holding onto for later, because it warns against a common error. Neither population could digest milk as adults. The lactase-persistence variant was rare in farmers and foragers alike, and stayed rare for another four to five thousand years, rising only in the Bronze and Iron Ages. Even the Yamnaya pastoralists, the supposed milk-drinking supermen of the steppe, carried it at a few percent at most. Genes and culture do not move in lockstep, and the people we are about to follow onto the grassland were not yet the people their reputation describes.

So by 5,000 BCE the arc had already remade Europe once, in the bodies of real migrants, before any Indo-European word was spoken on any steppe.

## IV. One arc, two farmers, as different as Europeans and East Asians

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Here is the structural fact that the whole homeland debate turns on, and it comes straight from the Reich lab.

The arc was not one population. At the moment farming began it held two genetically distinct West Eurasian lineages, each of which made its own independent leap from foraging to farming at opposite ends of the same highland belt. Lazaridis and colleagues established this in 2016, in a *Nature* paper on the origin of farming in the Near East, and they did not hedge the language. The first farmers of the Levant and the first farmers of the Zagros were, in their words, “strongly genetically differentiated,” and each “descended from local hunter-gatherers.” How differentiated? The press summary spelled it out: these early Near Eastern populations were “as different from one another as present-day Europeans are from East Asians.”

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5FOQ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F720bd786-823b-40c6-920a-686ac7f8960c_1360x1760.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5FOQ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F720bd786-823b-40c6-920a-686ac7f8960c_1360x1760.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

One arc, two farmers. The Anatolian pole flowed west and rebuilt Europe. The Iranian pole of the Zagros flowed east into Iran and India. Lazaridis's own lab found them as far apart as Europeans and East Asians.

Sit with that comparison. Inside one arc, before anyone moved anywhere, sat farming lineages as far apart as a Norwegian and a Han Chinese. The arc was not a single seed. It was a basin of deep diversity, and its two poles went on to seed two different halves of the world.

The **western pole**, the Anatolian Neolithic farmers, flooded into Europe as the Early European Farmers. When a geneticist says a modern Italian carries Anatolian farmer ancestry, this is the pole they mean.

The **eastern pole**, the Iran Neolithic farmers of the Zagros, around sites like Ganj Dareh, never went to Europe. They went east and south, into the Iranian plateau, into the great Central Asian Bronze Age civilization called the BMAC, onto the edges of the Indus Valley, and through those into the deep ancestry of nearly every modern South Asian.

Lazaridis 2016 even drew the arrows itself: farmers related to Anatolia “spread westward into Europe,” farmers related to Iran “spread northward into the Eurasian steppe,” and people related to both the Iranian farmers and the steppe pastoralists “spread eastward into South Asia.” That single sentence, written by the steppe camp’s own flagship lab, is the two-pole thesis in full. Europe’s western ancestry is the Anatolian pole. South Asia’s western ancestry is the Iranian pole. The two only fuse much later, and much further north, on the grassland. Which tells you what the grassland actually was.

There is a deeper layer under both poles. Lazaridis 2016 found that each of them drew roughly half its ancestry from a ghost lineage called **Basal Eurasian**, a population that split off before all other non-Africans diverged from one another and that carried little or no Neanderthal ancestry. Basal Eurasian sits in the south, in West Asia, not on any steppe. The deepest root of the West Eurasian family tree points down into the arc.

## V. The language is too old for the steppe

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The genes are half the case. Indo-European is fundamentally a language problem: one mother tongue, spoken somewhere at some time, whose daughters are now spoken by nearly half the human race. The reigning answer locates her on the Pontic-Caspian grassland around 4,000 to 3,500 BCE among horse-riding, wagon-driving pastoralists. The linguistics has never sat comfortably in that chair, and the person who has done the most to say so is **Paul Heggarty**.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2cG-!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2d2e75db-6ffb-44f5-ab68-75ab4f6930db_2626x4675.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2cG-!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2d2e75db-6ffb-44f5-ab68-75ab4f6930db_2626x4675.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Three models for the Indo-European homeland. The steppe original (B), the farming/arc model (C), and Heggarty's hybrid (D), in which the deepest roots sit in the arc around 8,100 years ago and the steppe is a later staging ground. Heggarty et al., Science 2023, Fig. 1.

In 2023, Heggarty and a large international team published in *Science* the most rigorous Bayesian dating of the Indo-European family yet attempted: 161 languages, 109 living and 52 ancient, scored on a carefully vetted database of core vocabulary, run through a statistical engine that estimates a family’s age from the rate its words diverge. The root came out at roughly **8,120 years before present**, around 6,100 BCE. That is two thousand years too old for a homeland that only got moving on the steppe around 4,000 BCE. A root that deep lands squarely in the Neolithic of the arc, at the time and place farming and its vocabulary were spreading outward.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Odl8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffa260d45-2938-42f0-9af5-2dc8b20ac7bd_1444x1002.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Odl8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffa260d45-2938-42f0-9af5-2dc8b20ac7bd_1444x1002.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Heggarty's dated tree of 161 Indo-European languages. The root falls around 8,120 years ago, two thousand years too deep for a steppe homeland. Heggarty et al., Science 2023.

Heggarty’s model is a hybrid. It grants that the steppe was a major secondary staging ground for the European branches. It locates the primary homeland, the place of the deepest splits, in the highlands south of the Caucasus. This is not a fringe position dreamed up to annoy Harvard. It revives a homeland that the linguists Gamkrelidze and Ivanov argued for on purely linguistic grounds back in the 1980s, well before anyone could read ancient DNA.

The method had a sharp side benefit worth naming, because it reframes how you read the oldest texts. Heggarty’s “sampled ancestors” approach shows that Vedic Sanskrit is a sibling of the modern Indic languages, not their ancestor, just as Classical Latin is a sibling of Romance rather than its parent. The diversification of these families is older than their oldest surviving scriptures. The Rigveda is not the beginning. It is one preserved branch of something already old.

And the model gets one thing right that the pure steppe story cannot. **Anatolian.**

## VI. Anatolian breaks the steppe-only model, and Lazaridis admits it

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Anatolian, the branch that includes Hittite, is the oldest and most divergent limb of Indo-European, the first to split from everything else. If the family was born on the steppe, then Hittite, spoken in the heart of Anatolia, should trace back to steppe migrants. It does not, and the people who proved it did not want to.

The flagship of the steppe hypothesis right now is **Lazaridis and colleagues, 2025, in** ***Nature***, “The genetic origin of the Indo-Europeans.” It is a serious and impressive paper: 428 ancient individuals, the densest genomic transect of steppe prehistory ever built, with David Anthony among the authors. It dates the formation of the core Yamnaya to **4,038 BCE**, traces an explosive Yamnaya expansion between roughly 3,750 and 3,350 BCE that fanned five thousand kilometers from Hungary to Kazakhstan with almost no mixing into the locals it rolled over, and proposes that the deepest unity, “Proto-Indo-Anatolian,” was spoken between 4,400 and 4,000 BCE by people on a steppe-edge gradient it names the Caucasus Lower Volga, or CLV, cline.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lOlW!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd00f335b-c02b-45e8-bea3-66e06abd18fc_2446x1520.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lOlW!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd00f335b-c02b-45e8-bea3-66e06abd18fc_2446x1520.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The gods of Hattusa, carved at Yazılıkaya. The Hittites spoke the oldest branch of Indo-European yet carried only about ten percent steppe ancestry. The first branch never left the arc.

Read at speed, that looks like the steppe homeland confirmed. Read closely, it is a retreat, and Heggarty said so in a *Nature* commentary in February 2025 with a title that did not pull punches: a beating of the retreat from the steppe hypothesis. Three things give the game away, and all three are in Lazaridis’s own data.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!m2vd!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7850bb72-5f69-4037-883e-a4d48c68ede8_962x340.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!m2vd!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7850bb72-5f69-4037-883e-a4d48c68ede8_962x340.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Even the Hittites, who spoke the oldest branch of Indo-European, carry only about ten percent steppe ancestry, arriving late and from the south by way of the Caucasus. The first branch of the family barely left the arc. Lazaridis et al., Nature 2025, Extended Data Fig. 1.

First, the direction of flow. The paper presents the CLV gradient as a neutral cline, as if ancestry sloshed evenly along it. But the southern, Caucasus-and-Zagros ancestry is densest at the southern end, around Aknashen near complete and Maikop close behind, and thins as you move north onto the steppe. That is the fingerprint of a population spreading from the south into the grassland, a founder effect radiating northward. The deep ancestry that built the steppe Indo-Europeans was itself moving up out of the arc. As Heggarty put it, the key movement heads north from the Caucasus, not south from the Volga.

Second, Anatolian. Lazaridis concedes in print that Bronze Age central Anatolians, the actual speakers of Hittite, carry only “at least a tenth,” about ten percent, of CLV steppe-related ancestry. He writes that “Anatolian is remarkable for its lack of steppe ancestry down to the Bronze Age.” The original steppe hypothesis claimed all branches, Anatolian included, came from the steppe. If the Hittites are ninety percent non-steppe, the oldest branch of the family did not come from the steppe, and once one branch is conceded, the burden on every other branch shifts. The 2022 Southern Arc papers from the same lab say it even more plainly: Anatolia before 3,000 BCE “derived almost entirely from local West Asian ancestry, with no significant steppe contribution.”

Third, the relabeling. Faced with an Anatolian branch that will not cooperate, the paper redraws the tree so that “Indo-European” now technically excludes Anatolian, which is folded into a deeper node called “Indo-Anatolian.” This lets you keep saying the Indo-Europeans came from the steppe while the inconvenient branch quietly exits through a door marked with a different word. The shot missed, so the goalposts moved.

There is fresh corroboration from this year, and I want to state it precisely because its headline is about something else. In April 2026, **Akbari and the Reich lab** published in *Nature* the largest ancient-DNA study ever assembled, 15,836 West Eurasian genomes, ten thousand of them new, a single paper that doubled the entire published record. Its subject is natural selection, not language homelands. But when the team traced a specific immune variant that rose sharply in Yamnaya-related populations, they found the selection had not begun on the steppe. It began earlier, south of the Caucasus, in people who carried no steppe ancestry at all. Even the biology we file under “steppe” keeps resolving, on inspection, to a source further south.

## VII. The steppe was a mixing floor, and it was half-southern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Push one level deeper and the cradle-versus-engine line gets impossible for the steppe partisans to hold, because the Yamnaya themselves were, by ancestry, substantially southern.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_pOF!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F76f5a059-d24c-4471-8c58-59ee40c93920_3078x1688.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!_pOF!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F76f5a059-d24c-4471-8c58-59ee40c93920_3078x1688.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

A Yamnaya kurgan burial at Boldești-Grădiștea on the lower Danube, the body contracted and dusted with red ochre. This is the western frontier of the expansion. By ancestry, though, the Yamnaya were already about forty percent southern. Excavation: The Yamnaya Impact on Prehistoric Europe project, University of Helsinki.

Take the lab’s own decomposition. At formation, Yamnaya is roughly **forty percent Caucasus hunter-gatherer and sixty percent Eastern European hunter-gatherer** (Lazaridis and colleagues, the 2022 Southern Arc study, 727 individuals). The Caucasus component, CHG, is a near-twin of the Iran Neolithic, the eastern pole of our arc. So before Yamnaya even exists around 4,000 BCE, something close to forty percent of it is already southern, already drawn up out of the highlands. Layer in the CLV model from the 2025 paper, where Yamnaya is downstream of that Caucasus-Lower-Volga population, and the steppe looks less like a wellspring and more like a basin where two arc-derived streams and a northern forager stream met, blended, and invented a mobile pastoral economy.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!G8oP!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d3fce3a-7834-4ce0-8bd8-5c24583a5839_1836x2376.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!G8oP!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d3fce3a-7834-4ce0-8bd8-5c24583a5839_1836x2376.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The steppe as a mixing floor. On this map of ancestry, the Yamnaya-ancestral clines sit between the northern hunter-gatherers and the southern poles of the arc, the Caucasus, Iran, and the Anatolian farmers. The grassland is where the arc's lineages recombined. Lazaridis et al., Nature 2025, Fig. 1.

The grassland supplied real things, and I am not waving them away: the horse, the wagon, the open road, and a demographic detonation that reshaped Europe and reached India. What it did not supply was the deep ancestry or, on Heggarty’s dating, the parent language. Both of those came from the arc. The steppe is where the family caught fire. It is not where the spark was struck.

## VIII. The receipt from home: zero steppe in the Indus Valley

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Turn to the eastern arm of the arc and the case stops being an argument about ancient Russians. It becomes a direct genetic receipt out of the Indian subcontinent.

In 2019, Shinde, Narasimhan, and colleagues published in *Cell* the first genome ever recovered from the Indus Valley Civilization. The individual, a woman from the cemetery at **Rakhigarhi** in Haryana, lived at the civilization’s mature peak, around 2,800 to 2,300 BCE. The paper’s title states the result without ornament: an ancient Harappan genome lacking ancestry from steppe pastoralists or Iranian farmers. She carried about half **Iran-related Neolithic** ancestry and about a tenth **AASI**, the deep indigenous South Asian hunter-gatherer lineage. Of steppe ancestry she carried, at the height of the Indus cities, statistically zero, ruled out at better than two standard deviations. The Yamnaya were galloping across Eurasia while she lived, and not one detectable thread of them had reached her.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!F8j8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F34bc7ffd-23e5-450c-a2c5-287d83a44891_1312x774.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!F8j8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F34bc7ffd-23e5-450c-a2c5-287d83a44891_1312x774.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Indus Valley at its height, around 2,500 BCE. The one genome from Rakhigarhi carries Iranian and indigenous ancestry and zero steppe.

The Iranian ancestry she did carry points the same direction the rest of this essay points. It is not the kind of Iranian ancestry that would have spread alongside Anatolian farmers. It is an early-diverging Iranian-plateau lineage that split off before farming was even invented, before 8,000 BCE. The western pole of South Asian ancestry does not trace to Anatolia and does not trace to the steppe. It traces to the Zagros Neolithic, the eastern arm of the arc, and to a branch of it so old it predates the farm.

The steppe ancestry did eventually arrive in South Asia. The same Narasimhan study, 523 ancient genomes, shows it: Central Asian steppe ancestry is absent before 2000 BCE, reaches the region around 2000 to 1500 BCE, after the Indus cities had already declined, and enters with a strong male bias, an estimated two to three steppe-carrying men for every woman, riding the R1a lineages that the later record tracks. It came late, it came partial, and it came as men marrying into a resident population whose foundation was already Iranian and AASI.

This is where the modern data closes the loop, and the closing is the part I find hardest to argue with. Three independent mega-studies, built on completely different samples, all reach the same verdict. Narasimhan 2019 with 523 ancient genomes. Kerdoncuff and colleagues in 2024 with 2,762 modern whole genomes, who put Iranian-farmer ancestry across the subcontinent at roughly thirty-five to seventy-five percent and steppe at a smaller, male-biased ten to forty percent. And the GenomeIndia atlas of 2026, nearly ten thousand whole genomes across more than eighty populations, where the R1a frequency tracks the western, steppe-derived component through the male line while the maternal lineages stay overwhelmingly local. Every dataset, ancient and modern, agrees: the largest western component in Indian ancestry is the Iranian pole, and the steppe is a late, male-mediated minority laid on top of it.

Then the line I keep returning to, because for me it settles the question of who is central. Open any West Eurasian genome from 9,000 BCE forward and Iran Neolithic ancestry is inside it. It is in the Anatolian farmers. It is in the Caucasus hunter-gatherers. It is in the Yamnaya, through the CHG. It is in the Indus periphery, in every modern Iranian and Kurd and Baloch and Gujarati and Brahmin and Tamil. The one population on Earth that carried Iran_N ancestry before anyone else carried any was the Zagros Neolithic population itself, because it was Iran_N. You cannot stand further upstream than being the source everyone else drank from.

## IX. The cradle of the gods

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The genetic and linguistic case stands on its own. But it would miss the part that makes the arc feel like a place rather than a smear of ancestry on a map. The arc was also where the religious imagination of half the world took its familiar shapes, and many of those shapes can be traced moving outward from it.

The deep-time evidence is the architecture itself. Göbekli Tepe’s rings of carved pillars, raised before the harvest. Çatalhöyük’s houses full of bull horns and plastered ancestor skulls and a goddess seated between two leopards. A jar from **Hajji Firuz Tepe** in the northern Zagros, dated to about 5,400 to 5,000 BCE, that Patrick McGovern’s chemical analysis showed had held resinated grape wine, the oldest such evidence in the Near East. The record has since been pushed back a few centuries by jars from Georgia in the South Caucasus, which is to say still inside the arc. Wine, the bull, the goddess, the ancestor cult, the sacred enclosure, all of it assembled in the arc thousands of years before any Indo-European word reached the steppe.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZqM8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F19f579a0-9c61-4109-af09-960a91018cf1_1600x1656.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZqM8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F19f579a0-9c61-4109-af09-960a91018cf1_1600x1656.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Dgpc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5678a3f6-9415-4b92-930d-fa7d714bac47_400x534.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Dgpc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5678a3f6-9415-4b92-930d-fa7d714bac47_400x534.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Two of the oldest wine vessels on Earth. Hajji Firuz in the Zagros (about 5,400 BCE, left) and Gadachrili Gora in Georgia (about 6,000 BCE, right). The record for the world's first wine has only ever moved between two ends of the same highland arc. Penn Museum / McGovern et al. 2017.

The traveling part needs care, because some of it is rock-solid and some of it is reaching, and I will tell you which is which.

The solid core is the comparative philology. Calvert Watkins spent a career showing that one mytheme, a hero or storm god slaying a serpent, recurs across the Indo-European world in phrasing so precisely parallel it must descend from a single Proto-Indo-European original. Indra kills Vritra in the Rigveda. The Hittite storm god Tarhunt kills the serpent Illuyanka, inside the arc. Apollo kills Python, Thor fights the world-serpent. The single sharpest receipt is that the slayer’s very name reconstructs across the Indo-Iranian split: Vedic **Vritrahan**, “Vritra-slayer,” is the exact cognate of Avestan **Verethragna**. The same god, killing the same dragon, in two languages that had only just parted. And the structure surfaces just outside the family, in the Bronze Age West Asian world the arc was embedded in: Baal kills Yam the sea in the Ugaritic texts, Marduk splits Tiamat in the Babylonian creation epic, and the Hebrew Bible keeps Yahweh defeating Leviathan and Rahab in Isaiah, Job, and the Psalms.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5gDu!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb077933b-6092-4f15-9197-fc50c8ef97d5_1117x1409.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!5gDu!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb077933b-6092-4f15-9197-fc50c8ef97d5_1117x1409.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Gustave Doré, "The Destruction of Leviathan," 1865. The storm god kills the serpent, here as Yahweh and Leviathan. The same scene is Indra slaying Vritra in the Rigveda, Tarhunt killing Illuyanka among the Hittites, and Marduk splitting Tiamat in Babylon. One myth, many languages, clustered in and around the arc.

A second structure runs even deeper. Bruce Lincoln reconstructed a Proto-Indo-European creation myth in which the first man, **Manu**, sacrifices his twin, **Yemo**, and a primal ox, and the cosmos is built from the pieces. The names survive across the family: Yemo gives Vedic Yama, Iranian Yima, Norse Ymir, and Roman Remus, while Manu gives Vedic Manu, Germanic Mannus, and the Tuisto of Tacitus. The homology survives too, the dismembered body mapped onto the world, flesh to earth, bone to stone, blood to water, eye to sun. You can read it in the Rigveda’s Purusha hymn, where the cosmic man is divided to make the social order, and in the Norse Grimnismal, where Ymir’s body becomes the land. One creation myth, told in a dozen daughter tongues.

A third pattern is the vocabulary of the holy itself. The luminous radiance that clothes gods and kings has a recognizable cognate-cluster across the whole zone: Hebrew **kavod**, Akkadian **melammu**, Vedic **tejas**, Iranian **khvarenah**, and Greek **doxa**, which is the word the Septuagint chose to render kavod, carrying the idea straight into Christianity. And the chaos-water at the opening of Genesis, **tehom**, the deep over which the spirit hovers, is the same word as Babylonian **Tiamat**, the sea-serpent that Marduk slays. The deep that the Hebrew creator tames is, etymologically, the dragon his neighbors killed.

The most consequential transmission runs through Iran. Bodily resurrection, heaven and hell as moral destinations, a named adversary called Satan, ranked armies of angels, the apocalypse, a coming savior: this furniture is thin in the oldest layers of the Hebrew Bible and dense in Iranian religion that predates the long Persian contact with the Jewish exiles. The mainstream reading, argued by Bruce Lincoln among others, is that this eschatology entered Western religion during the Persian period, out of the Iranian plateau, the eastern arm of the arc. The geography is exact. The cradle of farming and language was also the source of the Western afterlife.

There is even a clean fossil of the Indo-Iranian split inside this religious record. The Sanskrit **asura** and the Iranian **ahura** are the same word, and so are Vedic **deva** and Iranian **daeva**, but the two traditions reversed their values: where India’s devas are gods and some asuras turn demonic, Iran’s ahura is the supreme good and the daevas become demons. We can even watch the reform happen in stone. Xerxes left an inscription boasting that “where previously the daivas were worshipped, there I worshipped Ahuramazda.” One people, one vocabulary, split near the arc, and one side flipped the polarity of heaven.

Now the honest accounting, because a strong case names its own seams. The dragon-slayer formula, the Vritrahan-Verethragna cognate, the Manu-Yemo cosmogony, the kavod-melammu-tejas cluster, and the tehom-Tiamat identity are established comparative scholarship. The Iranian origin of Western eschatology is mainstream, debated at the edges. The larger claim, that all of this is one “religious kit” exported from a single cradle, is my synthesis, not a settled finding, and a careful scholar could pull on any individual thread. I offer the synthesis as a pattern worth taking seriously. The individual threads I offer as receipts. What I will stand on flatly is the geography. The first temple, the first ancestor cult, the first wine, the dragon-slaying storm god, the radiance-word, and the inherited afterlife all sit in the same highland crescent, in the same span of millennia, upstream of everyone.

## X. The honest objections, and why they do not sink the case

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

A case that only lists its own evidence is advertising, not argument. Here are the strongest objections, stated at full strength.

**The steppe authors themselves read their data the other way.** This is the most important one to be straight about. Shinde and Narasimhan do not think Rakhigarhi supports a southern homeland. They read the zero-steppe result as proof that steppe ancestry, and with it Indo-Aryan language, arrived later, after 2000 BCE, exactly as a steppe migration into India would predict. Their data is genuinely useful to the arc thesis on the question of the deep eastern pole. Their conclusions are not on my side, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest. What I am claiming is narrower and, I think, sturdier: the deep foundational ancestry of both Europe and South Asia is arc-derived, and the language root, on the best current linguistics, is older and more southern than the steppe model allows.

**The wheel and the wagon.** The single best card the steppe model holds is linguistic. The reconstructed Proto-Indo-European vocabulary includes solid words for wheel, axle, and wagon, and wheeled vehicles do not appear archaeologically until around 3,500 BCE. If the proto-language already had those words, it cannot be much older than the wheel, which collides head-on with Heggarty’s 8,120-year date. There are answers, that the wheel terms belong to a later, post-Anatolian layer spreading through an already-dispersing dialect network, and that linguistic paleontology is shakier than its fans admit, but I will not pretend the tension is resolved. It is the real soft spot in the early-and-southern dating, and an honest reader should hold it open.

**No steppe artifacts in India.** Critics note that the material culture of the steppe, Andronovo metalwork and pottery, does not show up in South Asia, which sits awkwardly beside a claimed genetic migration. The standard reply is that language and genes can spread through elite dominance without a full artifact package, and the male-biased ancestry pattern fits that. Both halves of this are worth keeping in view.

**The symmetry problem cuts at everyone.** Heggarty’s sharpest jab is that Indic speakers carry meaningful steppe ancestry while Iranic speakers carry very little, despite sharing a recent common Indo-Iranian ancestor, which is hard to square with a single steppe expansion driving both languages. Fair. But the mirror objection is also fair: if steppe migration fails to explain South Asia, why does it work so cleanly for Europe, where Corded Ware populations are something like three-quarters steppe? The honest position is that the steppe expansion was demographically real and enormous, and that being real and enormous is not the same as being the cradle. An engine can be the largest thing in the story and still not be the source.

None of these objections touches the spine of the case. Two divergent farmer poles in the arc, as different as Europeans from East Asians. Europe built from the western pole, South Asia from the eastern pole, the two fusing only later on the steppe. A language root too old for the grassland. An Anatolian branch the steppe model cannot keep. Iran Neolithic ancestry upstream of every West Eurasian on the planet. The objections nibble the edges. The center holds.

## XI. The arc

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Picture the whole thing at once. A crescent of highland country curving from the Aegean coast of Anatolia, through the Armenian heights, down the spine of the Zagros to the edge of the Iranian plateau. Eleven thousand years ago the people of that crescent invented farming at both of its ends, in two lineages so distinct they were as different from each other as a European is from an East Asian. They raised the first temples while they were still half wild, packed themselves into towns of bull horns and plastered skulls, pressed the first wine. The western pole walked and sailed into Europe and rebuilt it from the soil up. The eastern pole poured down into Iran and across to the Indus and laid the foundation of India. The deepest layers of their language went with them, the first branch never even leaving home, the rest drifting north onto the grass basin between the seas, where they met the horse and the wagon, fused into the Yamnaya, and detonated outward carrying daughter tongues that had already begun to part in the south.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7yaU!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa0de435a-efd3-45d7-b456-e1742dd59a1e_2376x1836.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7yaU!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa0de435a-efd3-45d7-b456-e1742dd59a1e_2376x1836.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The arc, one highland crescent from Anatolia through Armenia into the Zagros. The cradle of farming, of the gods, and of the deepest layers of Indo-European. Call it ZAA. Summary map after Lazaridis et al., Nature 2025.

The steppe was the engine. It was loud and fast and it changed the map of the world, and the scholars who study it are studying something real. But the cradle was the arc. The wheat came from there, and the word for father, and the storm god who kills the serpent, and the radiance on the face of the holy, and a very large share of the genome you are reading this with. The most striking part is that you do not have to take a heterodox scholar’s word for any of the genetics. The Reich lab measured the two poles and called them as far apart as Europeans and East Asians. Lazaridis drew the arrows from the arc into Europe and India. Anthony put his name on the paper that makes Yamnaya a downstream population of a Caucasus source. They were trying to map the steppe homeland. They mapped the road out of the arc instead.

The homeland was never the steppe. It was always, quietly, upstream.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Sources and further reading

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Farming origins and the Neolithic arc**

- Excavation and chronology of Göbekli Tepe (Klaus Schmidt and
  successors), Çatalhöyük (Ian Hodder and the Çatalhöyük Research
  Project), Jericho, and Ganj Dareh.

- Patrick McGovern, chemical residue analysis of the Hajji Firuz Tepe
  wine jars, University of Pennsylvania Museum: earliest Near Eastern
  grape wine, about 5,400 to 5,000 BCE, later superseded by South
  Caucasus finds near 6,000 BCE.

**The two poles and the genetics of the first farmers**

- Lazaridis et al. (2016), “Genomic insights into the origin of farming
  in the ancient Near East,” *Nature* 536:419 to 424. The Levant and
  Iran farmer poles “strongly genetically differentiated,” each from
  local hunter-gatherers, and “as different from one another as
  present-day Europeans are from East Asians.” Both poles roughly half
  Basal Eurasian. The directional sentence: Anatolian farmers west into
  Europe, Iranian farmers north into the steppe, both plus steppe east
  into South Asia.

- Haak et al. (2010, 2015) and Mathieson et al. (2015, *Nature*): early
  European farmer genomes, the migration into Europe, SLC24A5 and
  pigmentation, and the late rise of lactase persistence.

**Indo-European homeland, the two models**

- Lazaridis et al. (2025), “The genetic origin of the Indo-Europeans,”
  *Nature* 639:132 to 142. 428 ancient genomes (David Anthony among the
  authors); Yamnaya formation about 4,038 BCE; the CLV cline;
  Proto-Indo-Anatolian 4,400 to 4,000 BCE; Anatolian only about ten
  percent steppe ancestry.

- Lazaridis et al. (2022), “The genetic history of the Southern Arc,”
  *Science*, 727 individuals: Yamnaya formed about forty percent CHG and
  sixty percent EHG; Anatolia before 3,000 BCE “almost entirely from
  local West Asian ancestry, with no significant steppe contribution.”

- Heggarty et al. (2023), “Language trees with sampled ancestors support
  a hybrid model for the origin of Indo-European languages,”
  *Science* 381. 161 languages; root about 8,120 years before present;
  primary homeland south of the Caucasus; Vedic and Latin as siblings,
  not ancestors, of their living families.

- Heggarty (2025), “Beating the retreat from the Steppe hypothesis,”
  *Nature* commentary on Lazaridis 2025.

- Akbari et al. (2026), “Ancient DNA reveals pervasive directional
  selection across West Eurasia,” *Nature*. 15,836 genomes; directional
  selection on a Yamnaya-associated immune variant beginning south of
  the Caucasus, before steppe ancestry.

- Background, the steppe case at full strength: David W. Anthony, *The
  Horse, the Wheel, and Language* (2007), the standard statement of the
  wheel-and-wagon argument that any southern-and-early dating must
  answer.

**South Asia**

- Shinde et al. (2019, *Cell* 179:729): the Rakhigarhi Harappan genome,
  about half Iran Neolithic and a tenth AASI, statistically zero steppe
  at the IVC peak; the Iranian component diverging before 8,000 BCE. The
  authors read the result as consistent with a later steppe arrival.

- Narasimhan et al. (2019, *Science* 365): the formation of populations
  in South and Central Asia; steppe ancestry absent before 2000 BCE,
  arriving roughly 2000 to 1500 BCE with a two-to-three male bias.

- Kerdoncuff et al. (2024): 2,762 whole genomes; Iranian-farmer ancestry
  roughly thirty-five to seventy-five percent across the cline, steppe a
  smaller male-biased minority.

- GenomeIndia consortium (2026): nearly 10,000 whole genomes, more than
  80 populations; the western steppe-derived component male-biased via
  R1a while maternal lineages stay local.

**Religion and comparative myth**

- Calvert Watkins, *How to Kill a Dragon* (Oxford, 1995): the
  Proto-Indo-European dragon-slayer formula; the Vritrahan and
  Verethragna cognate.

- Bruce Lincoln, *Myth, Cosmos, and Society* and *Death, War, and
  Sacrifice*: the Manu and Yemo creation myth and its cognates; later
  work on Iranian religion and the transmission of eschatology.

- Benjamin Sommer, *The Bodies of God and the World of Ancient Israel*
  (Cambridge, 2009): divine fluidity and embodiment in the Bronze Age
  West Asian world.

- Mark S. Smith, *The Early History of God* and *The Origins of Biblical
  Monotheism*: the Northwest Semitic and Israelite background, including
  the kavod and melammu radiance vocabulary.

*A note on epistemic standing. The genetic and linguistic claims in sections I through VIII rest on peer-reviewed primary literature, much of it produced by scholars who favor the steppe hypothesis and whose data I am reading against their own conclusions. The religious-transmission argument in section IX rests on solid individual receipts (the dragon-slayer formula, the Vritrahan-Verethragna cognate, the Manu-Yemo cosmogony, the kavod cluster, the tehom-Tiamat identity, the Iranian sources of Western eschatology) but the claim that they form a single exported “religious kit” is an interpretive synthesis, offered as a pattern and not a proof.*

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Subscribe

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

5

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
